title: Scorecards: Building a Performance Evaluation System for Your Construction Business
summary: Erin Stetzer led a deep dive into her scorecard system for evaluating trades, architects, designers, and project managers, framing it as the heartbeat of a well-run construction business. The session used sports analogies to explain why ongoing performance tracking matters, and opened into a rich Q&A covering real participant challenges with architect communication, onboarding new PMs, sub relationships, and weekly reporting systems. Participants also explored supporting tools like Builder Trend, four-week calendars, and weekly project reports as companion systems to scorecards.

teachings.concept: Know the Game Before You Can Keep Score
teachings.explanation: Before implementing scorecards, a business owner must first identify what business they are in (spec building, remodeling, new construction, development), how to play that game, what result they want, and commit to playing all out. Scorecards only function within an already-operational engine.
teachings.quote: First things first, you have to know the game you're in. And that's why we're all here. People have different business models. But you have to identify before you even can get to a scorecard the game you're in.

teachings.explanation: Just as sports coaches review game film to identify patterns, weaknesses, and improvements, business owners should use scorecards as a structured way to observe and evaluate performance over time. The act of consistently reviewing creates compounding progress.
teachings.quote: You have to watch the game film at the end, because that's the one sure way that you can absolutely make positive, positive progress. If you're honest with yourself and your team, that's the one sure way you can do this.

teachings.concept: Scorecards Set the Tone Before Work Begins
teachings.explanation: Sharing scorecards with a new architect, trade, or designer at the start of a relationship establishes mutual expectations and a framework for respectful collaboration. It prevents misalignment and creates a shared language for accountability.
teachings.quote: Before we start a project, I show the scorecard. I said, these are the things that we're looking for in a partner.

teachings.concept: Separate the People from the Problem
teachings.explanation: When conflicts arise with partners or trades, emotionally separating the individual from the issue allows for more productive conversations. The problem can be addressed without personalizing it, which preserves the relationship and leads to better resolution.
teachings.quote: Whenever I find myself riled up about something, I always separate the people from the problem. And in this case, the people are over here, but this is the problem.

teachings.explanation: By filling out scorecards regularly, owners and managers begin to memorize the criteria and can identify when something is off track quickly and instinctively. Patterns of low scores on a line item signal the need for a direct conversation.
teachings.quote: If it's a two on that line item for four weeks, that requires a conversation with the team. So the idea is to fill it out, recognize the pattern early, address where we're too low, and get it higher.

teachings.explanation: When used actively, scorecards shift the default response to underperformance from firing someone to finding a solution together. The example of the painter who lost staff illustrates how early intervention through scorecard awareness led to a successful outcome rather than a crisis.
teachings.quote: It sets the tone for resolution as opposed to fire the person. And now I got to find another person, right? So it sets the tone for resolution.

teachings.concept: Weekly Reports as a Project Management Non-Negotiable
teachings.explanation: Requiring project managers to write detailed weekly reports forces them to think through the full state of the project, which improves their decision-making during the week and reduces the need to escalate to the owner. The discipline of writing the report builds competence.
teachings.quote: If I force them to do this and write their novel, they now are in a position to know how to make decisions during the week and they don't have to call me. So that's the beauty of the weekly report.

teachings.concept: Pilot Program Hiring Over Formal Offers
teachings.explanation: Rather than making formal job offers, Erin runs six-to-eight week paid pilot engagements on a 1099 basis, allowing both parties to evaluate fit before committing. This reduces hiring risk and surfaces the right people through demonstrated performance.
teachings.quote: It's not a formal offer. I'm going to throw you into two remodels. Show me what you have. And I have another person on the team watching them and I'm watching them and observing them and asking them questions for six to eight weeks.

actionable_advice: Show the scorecard to new architects, designers, and trades at the very first meeting before any project begins, so expectations are established from day one.
When experiencing communication frustration with a partner, take a 24-hour break before reaching out to ensure the conversation comes from a productive place rather than frustration.
Call the issue out directly but respectfully: share your scorecard observations and ask the partner how they would like to resolve the misalignment together.
Fill out scorecards consistently, ideally weekly, especially when onboarding a new system. Review them every Sunday night as part of weekly planning.

Require project managers to submit weekly reports covering work performed, work planned, material orders, approvals received, modifications, decisions needed from clients and designers, anticipated problems, and a red hot list of urgent items.
Have project managers post walkthrough videos to a shared cloud folder so owners can monitor how PMs think, observe, and make decisions without being on site daily.

When a trade is underperforming, have an honest conversation, give them a chance to course-correct with a specific plan, and if needed, ask them to bring in a subcontractor they manage rather than terminating the relationship outright.
Ask a new project manager to present their own build process and schedule to you rather than handing them yours, so you can identify their baseline and find natural insertion points for your systems.
Use a four-week rolling calendar shared with clients so everyone can anticipate upcoming phases and surface any last-minute change requests before work begins.
When onboarding a new hire, structure the engagement as a 1099 pilot over six to eight weeks with a guaranteed monthly amount, and have another team member observe alongside you.
client_challenges: David is working with a new architect firm he recommended to clients, but is experiencing poor communication with three-day response delays and feeling that his expectations and process are not being respected.

Tammy has hired a new project manager for new construction after self-managing, and wants a structured onboarding system with checklists and procurement prompts to ensure alignment without micromanaging.
Denise wants to understand how Erin structures her team roles and who is responsible for implementing and maintaining scorecard systems so she can model it for her own company.
Connor has recently moved to a new location and is starting fresh with all new subcontractors, raising the question of when and how to introduce scorecards in new sub relationships.
Ashley is concerned about trades communicating directly with clients when she is not present, particularly when schedule changes or off-track situations arise that require careful client management.

Brooke raised the question of how to transition from the scorecard introduction phase to actual project collaboration, and how to vet a new partner's ability to perform before committing to working together.
David asked how the weekly project report relates to OAC meeting agendas and whether emailed reports are actually being read and acted on by architects and clients.
A participant asked how owners find the time to implement all these systems given the daily demands of running a construction business.
notable_quotes: On scorecards as a business foundation: 'Scorecards are literally the heartbeat of our business.'
On staying engaged as an owner: 'A lot of owners step away. And to me, that is a major, major, major mistake. You got to stay in the trenches.'
On honest accountability: 'Thank God you didn't lie to me. If you had lied to me and said something else, that would have been your last day.'

On hiring great people before you can afford them: 'I said I can't hire any project managers who don't have 10 years of residential experience with an architect. I know a designer, I know. And then that positioned me — I trust them in the field.'

On the weekly report's origin: 'That weekly report, don't take it lightly. It literally was built off of a lot of pain, a lot of not getting paid, a lot of awkward conversations at the end of a project that I set myself up for. This alleviates it.'
On finding great team members: 'Everything you guys are seeing is not Aaron Stetzer staying up all night creating systems. It's like our team is the machine.'
